context: The Bible tells us in Leviticus 19, in verse number 15, he shall do no unrighteousness in judgment. Thou shalt not respect the person of the poor, nor honor the person of the mighty,
point_summary: Justice, as a biblical mandate rooted in Leviticus 19:15, demands impartiality toward both poor and powerful, a standard the sermon frames as a governing principle for evaluating candidates at the ballot box.

context: Now that would have been, according to the book of Leviticus, verse number 22, would have been 40 days after the birth of Christ. That's fulfilling the law.
point_summary: Leviticus 12 establishes the purification law that required Mary and Joseph to bring Jesus to Jerusalem forty days after his birth, which the sermon identifies as the legal context fulfilled in Luke 2.

context: be holy, for I am holy
point_summary: God's call to holiness in Leviticus—"be holy, for I am holy"—is cited as the scriptural foundation for the New Testament summons to walk not in uncleanness but in holiness.

context: jot it down to read Leviticus 26 later.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: God said in Leviticus, and then it's repeated in the New Testament, be holy, why? For I am holy.
point_summary: Holiness as a divine standard—"be holy, for I am holy"—grounds the church's responsibility to maintain purity and not tolerate evil within its membership.

context: Specifically given the truth of not performing these things, this abomination before the Lord in Leviticus 18 v. 21.
point_summary: Micah 6:7's rhetorical question about sacrificing one's firstborn gains sharp historical weight when read against Leviticus 18:21's explicit prohibition, since King Ahaz literally violated that command by offering his son to Molech.

context: Look at Leviticus 16. You can see it. With the blood of the goat.
point_summary: Leviticus 16's Day of Atonement required the high priest to enter twice and repeat annually, contrasting with Christ's single, sufficient entry as the sinless substitute who needed no offering for His own sin.

reference_text: the Word of God warns about offering your children to Molech
context: You know the Word of God warns about offering your children to Molech?
point_summary: Ahaz's burning of his children in the fire is connected to the biblical warning against offering children to Molech, illustrating how idolatrous influence leads to the very abominations God condemned among the Canaanites.

context: be holy for I am holy
point_summary: God's call to holiness in Leviticus 11:44 — "be holy, for I am holy" — is cited as the scriptural foundation for the sermon's theme of self-examination in preparation for revival.

context: we read it in the book of Leviticus, but we also read it in the book of 1 Peter, that we are to be holy. Why? Because our God is holy.
point_summary: God's holiness, declared thrice by the seraphim in Isaiah 6, grounds the Levitical command that His people likewise be holy.

context: we're to be holy, why? Because He is holy. And we see that, of course, first mentioned in the book of Leviticus
point_summary: God's holiness is His most prominent moral attribute—perfectly pure and complete in moral character—and it is the very basis for His call that His people likewise be holy.

context: ...thou shalt love thy neighbor as thyself...
point_summary: The commandment to love one's neighbor, rooted in Leviticus 19:18, is identified as the "old commandment" John references—a truth already known from the Pentateuch—against which the newness of Christ's standard in John 13:34 is contrasted.

context: ...thou shalt love him as thyself...
point_summary: Leviticus 19:34 is cited alongside 19:18 to establish that the command to love—extending even to strangers—was already present in the Pentateuch, grounding John's "old commandment" in Israel's ancient Scripture.

context: but go over to the book of Leviticus, if you will, in the 26th chapter. Leviticus 26. It's just a little bit before 630. There are 46 verses in this chapter, and I'm going to be honest with you, I don't know where to cut it off. I'm going to read the entire thing because it's necessary. Because we're going to see that this situation in Joel is the direct result of sin. In Leviticus 26, I want you to notice the positive nature of the first 13 verses
point_summary: Leviticus 26 is read in full to establish that the agricultural devastation Joel describes is the direct covenantal consequence of Israel's sin.

context: ...Ye shall make you no idols or a graven image, neither rear you up a standing image, neither shall you set up any image of stone in your land to bow down unto it, for I am the Lord your God.
point_summary: Leviticus 26's conditional "if-then" structure establishes that Israel's covenant obedience would bring blessing, providing the biblical framework for understanding Joel's agricultural devastation as the direct result of sin.

context: You shall keep My Sabbaths and reverence My sanctuary. I am the Lord.
point_summary: Leviticus 26's conditional "if-then" structure establishes that the agricultural devastation described in Joel is the direct result of Israel's failure to walk in God's statutes and keep His commandments.

context: If ye walk in My statutes and keep My commandments and do them...
point_summary: Leviticus 26:3's conditional "if" introduces a classic if-then covenant structure: obedience to God's statutes brings promised blessing, framing Joel's agricultural devastation as the consequence of broken covenant.

context: Then I will give you rain in due season, and the land shall yield her increase, and the trees of the field shall yield their fruit.
point_summary: The agricultural abundance promised in Leviticus 26:4 is presented as the "then" half of a classic biblical if-then covenant: obedience to God's statutes yields rain and harvest, making its absence in Joel a direct consequence of sin.

context: Walk in My statutes, keep My commandments, and do them.
point_summary: Leviticus 26:3's opening "if" and verse 4's answering "then" establish a conditional covenant structure whose promised agricultural blessings—rain, harvest, fruitfulness—were visibly absent in Joel 1.

context: I will give you rain in due season, and the land shall yield her increase, and the trees of the field shall yield their fruit.
point_summary: Leviticus 26:4's "then" answers the conditional "if" of verse 3, forming a classic covenant promise in which God pledges rain and agricultural abundance contingent on Israel's obedience—blessings conspicuously absent in Joel 1.

context: And your threshing shall reach unto the vintage, and the vintage shall reach unto the sowing time, and ye shall eat your bread to the full and dwell in your land safely.
point_summary: Leviticus 26:5's promise of unbroken agricultural abundance—threshing reaching vintage, vintage reaching sowing—stands as the covenant blessing Israel forfeited by the time of Joel 1's devastation.

context: And I will give peace in the land, and ye shall lie down, and none shall make you afraid.
point_summary: Leviticus 26:6's promise of peace, safety, and fruitfulness forms the conditional "if-then" backdrop against which Joel 1's withered harvests and broken land expose Israel's covenant disobedience.

context: And ye shall chase your enemies, and they shall fall before you by the sword.
point_summary: The blessings of Leviticus 26's classic if-then covenant structure—rain, fruitful harvests, peace, and military victory—stand as the obedience-conditioned promises conspicuously absent in Joel 1's devastated land.

context: And five of you shall chase an hundred, and an hundred of you shall put ten thousand to fight, and your enemies shall fall before you by the sword.
point_summary: Leviticus 26's classic if-then structure shows that God's promised blessings of fruitful harvests and military victory were conditional on Israel's obedience—blessings conspicuously absent in Joel 1.

context: For I will have respect unto you and make you fruitful and multiply you and establish My covenant with you.
point_summary: Leviticus 26's classic if-then structure establishes that God's covenant blessings—fruitful harvests, peace, and multiplied increase—were conditioned on Israel's obedience, blessings conspicuously absent in Joel 1.

context: And I will set My tabernacle among you, and My soul shall not abhor you.
point_summary: The blessings of Leviticus 26—rain, fruitfulness, peace, and God walking among His people—are structured as an if-then covenant promise whose absence in Joel 1 signals Israel's disobedience.

context: And I will walk among you and will be your God, and ye shall be My people.
point_summary: The blessings of Leviticus 26—rain, fruitfulness, peace, and God walking among His people—are framed as an if-then covenant promise whose fulfillment depends entirely on Israel's obedience.

context: I am the Lord your God, which brought you forth out of the land of Egypt, that you should not be their bondmen, and I have broken the bands of your yoke and made you go upright.
point_summary: The blessings of Leviticus 26 are structured as an if-then covenant promise, making God's favor—rain, fruitfulness, peace, and His presence—entirely dependent on Israel's obedience.

context: Praise God for the first part of Leviticus 26. But if you refuse, if you disobey, if you abhor, if you despise, then He says in the same way, the same promise, the same magnitude of the promise of v. 4, He gives in v. 16
point_summary: The blessings promised in Leviticus 26:4 are matched in equal magnitude by the curses of verses 16 and following, showing that God's covenant faithfulness operates symmetrically in both reward and chastening.

context: He gives in v. 16, I also will do this unto you. I will even appoint over you terror, consumption, the burning egg-yew that shall consume the eyes and cause sorrow of heart.
point_summary: God's covenant curses in Leviticus 26:16—terror, consumption, fruitless labor—carry the same covenantal weight as the blessings, and are given not for destruction but to drive His people back to Himself.

reference_text: if you will not yet for all this hearken unto Me, then I will punish you seven times more for your sins
context: And He says, if I do all of these things, and you still, for all of this, it says, and if you will not yet for all this hearken unto Me, then I will punish you seven times more for your sins.
point_summary: Divine chastening in Leviticus 26 is shown to escalate in severity—seven times more—precisely because its purpose is repentance and return, not mere punishment.

reference_text: And I will break the pride of your power, and I will make your heaven as iron, and your earth as brass
context: V. 19, And I will break the pride of your power, and I will make your heaven as iron, and your earth as brass.
point_summary: God's escalating covenant curses in Leviticus 26—breaking the pride of power, making heaven iron and earth brass—demonstrate that agricultural failure is divine chastening designed to drive His people back to Him.

reference_text: And your strength shall be spent in vain, for your land shall not yield her increase, neither shall the trees of the land yield their fruits
context: And your strength shall be spent in vain, for your land shall not yield her increase, neither shall the trees of the land yield their fruits.
point_summary: God's covenant curses in Leviticus 26 escalate in sevenfold waves precisely to drive His people back to Him, illustrating that divine chastening is purposeful, not merely punitive.

reference_text: And if you walk contrary unto Me, and will not hearken unto Me, I will bring seven times more
context: And if you walk contrary unto Me, and will not hearken unto Me, I will bring seven times more.
point_summary: Divine chastening in Leviticus 26 is shown to escalate in severity—seven times more—precisely because its purpose is repentance and return, not mere punishment.

reference_text: I will also send wild beasts among you which shall rob you of your children and destroy your cattle
context: I will also send wild beasts among you which shall rob you of your children and destroy your cattle
point_summary: God's escalating covenant curses in Leviticus 26—including wild beasts, crop failure, and desolation—are presented as purposeful chastening designed to drive His people back to Him.

context: and make you few in number, and your highways shall be desolate.
point_summary: God's escalating covenant judgments in Leviticus 26—wild beasts, desolation, and multiplied plagues—are disciplinary in intent, designed to drive His people back to Himself rather than to express mere wrath.

context: And if you will not be reformed by Me by these things, but will walk contrary unto Me,
point_summary: God's escalating covenant discipline—punishing "seven times more"—is not the action of a mean, angry God but a persistent call for His people to return to Him.

context: then will I also walk contrary unto you and will punish you yet seven times more for your sins.
point_summary: God's escalating discipline—punishing "seven times more"—is not the action of a mean, angry God but a persistent call for His people to return to Him.

context: And I will bring a sword upon you that shall avenge the quarrel of My covenant.
point_summary: God's escalating covenant judgments — sword, pestilence, and enemy defeat — are not expressions of divine cruelty but measured disciplines designed to drive His people back to Himself.

context: When I have broken the staff of your bread, men and women shall bake your bread in one oven and they shall deliver you your bread again by weight, and you shall eat and not be satisfied.
point_summary: God's escalating covenant judgments in Leviticus 26—including broken bread-staff and unsatisfied eating—are framed as intensifying divine discipline designed to drive His people back to Him, not expressions of arbitrary wrath.

context: And if you will not for all this hearken unto Me, but walk contrary unto Me,
point_summary: God's escalating discipline — punishing "seven times more" at each stage of continued rebellion — is covenant chastisement designed not for destruction but to drive His people back to Himself.

context: then I will walk contrary unto you also in fury, and I even I will chastise you seven times for your sins.
point_summary: God's escalating discipline — punishing "seven times more" at each stage of continued rebellion — is covenant chastisement designed to drive His people back to Him, not evidence of divine cruelty.

context: and you shall eat the flesh of your sons, and the flesh of your daughters shall you eat.
point_summary: Escalating covenant curses in Leviticus 26—including the horror of cannibalism fulfilled in 2 Kings—are presented as God intensifying judgment to drive His people back to Himself.

context: and I will destroy your high places and cut down your images and cast your carcasses upon the carcasses of your idols and My soul shall abhor you
point_summary: God's threatened judgment in Leviticus 26:30—destroying high places, desolating cities, scattering Israel—was literally fulfilled in the Babylonian captivity because the people did not return to Him.

context: and I will make your cities waste and bring your sanctuaries unto desolation and I will not smell the savor of your sweet odors
point_summary: God's threatened judgment of making cities waste and sanctuaries desolate was literally fulfilled in the Babylonian captivity because Israel did not return to Him.

context: and I will bring the land into desolation and your enemies which dwell therein shall be astonished at it
point_summary: God's covenant warnings of escalating judgment — including desolation of the land and scattering among the nations — were literally fulfilled in the Babylonian captivity because Israel persistently refused to return to Him.

context: and I will scatter you among the heathen
point_summary: God's threatened judgment of scattering Israel among the nations (Lev. 26:33) was literally fulfilled in the Babylonian captivity, cited here as evidence that covenant warnings carry real historical consequences.

context: then shall the land enjoy her sabbaths as long as it lieth desolate
point_summary: The land's enforced sabbath rest during Judah's seventy-year Babylonian captivity fulfilled Leviticus 26:34 precisely, explaining both Jeremiah's prophecy and Daniel's understanding in Daniel 9.

context: as long as it lieth desolate, it shall rest because it did not rest in your sabbaths
point_summary: The land's forced sabbath rest during Babylon's 70-year captivity was God's direct fulfillment of Leviticus 26, compensating for the sabbaths Israel had neglected while dwelling in it.

context: I will send a faintness into your hearts and the lands of their enemies and the sound of a shaken leaf shall chase them and they shall flee as fleeing from a sword and they shall fall when none pursueth
point_summary: God's covenant curse of sending faintness into the hearts of disobedient Israel, so that a shaken leaf would chase them, is read as fulfilled covenant judgment connected to the Babylonian captivity.

context: and they shall fall one upon another as it were before a sword when none pursueth and you shall have no power to stand before your enemies
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: and you shall perish among the heathen and the land of your enemies shall eat you up
point_summary: Leviticus 26:38's warning that Israel would "perish among the heathen" was fulfilled historically in the Babylonian captivity, directly tying the 70-year exile to covenant disobedience outlined in this chapter.

context: And they that are left of you shall pine away in their iniquity in your enemies' lands and also in the iniquities of their fathers shall they pine away with them
point_summary: Those who pine away in exile in their enemies' lands do so in their own iniquity and their fathers', yet confession and humbled hearts open the door to God's covenant remembrance.

context: if they shall confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers with their trespass which they trespassed against me and that also they have walked contrary unto me
point_summary: Leviticus 26:40's call to confess iniquity directly parallels Daniel's prayer in Daniel 9, showing the covenantal pathway from judgment to restoration through humble acknowledgment of sin.

context: and that I also have walked contrary unto them and have brought them into the land of their enemies. If then their uncircumcised hearts be humbled and they then accept of the punishment of their iniquity
point_summary: Leviticus 26:41's condition of humbled, uncircumcised hearts accepting punishment mirrors Daniel's prayer in Daniel 9, showing the covenantal pathway from exile back to divine remembrance.

context: then will I remember my covenant with Jacob and also my covenant with Isaac and also my covenant with Abraham will I remember and I will remember the land.
point_summary: God's covenant faithfulness to remember Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob even amid judgment—conditional on Israel's confession and humbled hearts—grounds the sermon's claim that God's unchanging character applies beyond Israel's specific context.

context: Verse 43, the land also shall be left of them and shall enjoy her sabbaths while she lieth desolate without them and they shall accept of the punishment of their iniquity because even because they despised my judgments and because their soul abhorred my statutes
point_summary: Leviticus 26:43 is read as the covenantal backdrop to Joel's locust judgment, showing that land desolation and national punishment were always the prescribed consequence for despising God's statutes—while God's covenant faithfulness remains intact.

context: and yet for all that when they be in the land of their enemies, I will not cast them away, neither will I abhor them to destroy them utterly and to break my covenant with them for I am the Lord their God.
point_summary: Even in covenant judgment and exile, God's unchanging character guarantees He will not utterly cast away or destroy His people, a principle the sermon applies beyond Israel to any nation under a holy God.

context: But I will for their sakes remember the covenant of their ancestors whom I brought forth out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the heathen that I might be their God, I am the Lord.
point_summary: Even as covenant curses fall on Israel, God's unchanging character—not casting away or utterly destroying his people—grounds the sermon's warning that the same holy God holds all nations accountable.

context: The solution that was given in Leviticus 26 near the end of the chapter, as we mentioned, is that turning back, that repentance, that acknowledgement of the sin, the iniquity, the transgression, and turning back to God, a confession
point_summary: Leviticus 26's closing provision—that repentance, confession, and acknowledgment of sin opens the way back to God's blessing—is presented as the scriptural solution mirrored in Joel 1:13–14.

context: We don't have time tonight, because we read all of Leviticus 26.
point_summary: Leviticus 26 is cited as background reading for the evening, establishing the covenantal framework of sin and consequence that parallels both David's loss of joy and Joel's national devastation.

context: Leviticus 26, they should read that in Congress every day next week.
point_summary: Leviticus 26 is invoked as a reminder that God is holy and that His people—and nations—stand under His judgment when they depart from Him.

context: he says to them over and over that he's holy.
point_summary: God's repeated declaration of His own holiness throughout Leviticus underlies the New Testament call to holiness, with Leviticus functioning as the instruction manual for approaching a holy God after exodus from the world.

context: Well, actually, if you go back to the law, it condemns both he and she. Amen? Leviticus. Just go back and read it.
point_summary: Leviticus's law on adultery condemned both parties equally, a textual detail invoked to expose the accusers' selective application of the law against the woman alone.

context: Notice what the Scriptures say if you're taking notes in verses 1-7 of Leviticus 25
point_summary: The Sabbath-year legislation of Leviticus 25 is framed as a test of faith and obedience whose underlying principles remain applicable beyond its original Israelite context.

context: six years thou shalt sow thy field, and six years thou shalt prune thy vineyard, and gather in the fruit thereof
point_summary: The six years of sowing, pruning, and harvesting in verse 3 establish the pattern of faithful labor that frames the seventh-year Sabbath rest as an act of trust in God's provision.

context: In the seventh year shall be a Sabbath of rest unto the land, a Sabbath for the Lord. Thou shalt neither sow thy field, nor prune thy vineyard
point_summary: The seventh year's complete cessation of sowing, pruning, and reaping constituted a Sabbath belonging to the Lord, marking the land itself as subject to divine rest and ownership.

context: you would find that the children of Israel never consistently obeyed the instruction that we read in Leviticus 25.
point_summary: God's omniscience—He knows what His people need before they ask—grounds the call to trust and obey the Sabbath-year command in Leviticus 25, and Israel's failure to do so meant missing the visible provision God had promised.

context: And a couple of passages later in Leviticus chapter number 26, we are in 25, it's a lengthy passage but in Leviticus 26 in verses 33 and 34, I want you to see what God says
point_summary: Israel's disobedience regarding the sabbath year brought the covenant judgment of scattering among the nations, the very consequence God warned would follow failure to keep His statutes.

context: And a couple of passages later in Leviticus chapter number 26, we are in 25, it's a lengthy passage but in Leviticus 26 in verses 33 and 34, I want you to see what God says
point_summary: Israel's failure to observe the sabbatical year brought the judgment of exile, so that the land finally received by force the sabbaths it had been denied through disobedience.

context: and I will scatter you among the heathen and will draw out a sword after you and your land shall be desolate and your cities waste.
point_summary: Disobedience to the Sabbath-year law carried a covenant consequence: the land would receive its forfeited Sabbaths through Israel's exile, fulfilled literally in the seventy-year Babylonian captivity.

context: It was 70 years because of a lack of obedience to this particular instruction that we're reading of in Leviticus 25.
point_summary: Disobedience to the Sabbath-year law of Leviticus 25 carried real consequences—Israel's seventy-year Babylonian captivity was God's land receiving the Sabbaths they had withheld through generations of unfaithfulness.

context: be holy, for I am holy
point_summary: Holiness as God's own character — "be holy, for I am holy" — grounds the call to upright behavior and good conscience that believers are to maintain in all manner of life.

context: ye shall make you no idols nor graven image, neither rear you up a standing image, neither shall you set up an image of stone in your land, to bow down unto it, for I am the Lord your God.
point_summary: The three Hebrews' refusal to bow was grounded not in emotion but in the explicit biblical command against idolatry, giving their stand the unchanging stability of divine principle.

context: If you go back to the book of Leviticus in chapters 13 and 14, you'll find how that the priest were to investigate
point_summary: Leviticus 13–14's priestly regulations for diagnosing leprosy illuminate why Gehazi, described as "white as snow," may paradoxically have been considered ceremonially clean under Mosaic law.

context: If you go back to the book of Leviticus in chapters 13 and 14, you'll find how that the priest were to investigate
point_summary: Leviticus 13–14's priestly regulations for diagnosing leprosy illuminate why Gehazi, struck "white as snow," may paradoxically have been considered ceremonially clean yet still under judgment.

context: Then the priest shall consider and behold, if the leprosy have covered all his flesh, he shall pronounce him clean that hath the plague. It is all turned white, he is clean.
point_summary: Leviticus 13:13's provision that a leper covered entirely in white is pronounced clean illuminates how Gehazi, though cursed with Naaman's leprosy, could still appear before the king.

context: We know that this book of Leviticus that I've had you turn to this evening that it's here in the book of Leviticus that God will deal with the importance of holy living and the importance of living in a manner that would please our God and our Savior.
point_summary: Leviticus is introduced as the biblical locus where God addresses holy living and conduct pleasing to Him, set within a broader call to separation from surrounding wickedness.

context: notice in verse number one of Leviticus 18 the Bible says this and the Lord spake unto Moses saying
point_summary: God's declaration "I am the Lord your God" in Leviticus 18:1 establishes divine authority as the foundational basis for the moral commands that follow, set against a backdrop of legislated wickedness.

context: ye shall do my judgments and keep mine ordinances to walk therein. I am the Lord your God.
point_summary: God's commands in Leviticus 18:4 carry binding authority because the one issuing them is the Creator of all things, whose position as Lord establishes his right to set the moral standard.

context: ye shall therefore keep my statutes and my judgments, which if a man do, he shall live in them. I am the Lord.
point_summary: God's authority as Creator grounds his right to establish moral standards, with the repeated declaration "I am the Lord your God" in verse 5 reinforcing that his commands carry divine weight.

context: God says here in Leviticus 18, the passage in which we've used as our study this evening, in verse number five, He says this, "'Which if a man do, he shall live in them. "'I am the Lord.'"
point_summary: Obeying God's commandments is presented as life-giving and advantageous, with "he shall live in them" grounding the call to delight in and meditate upon God's Word for our own good.

context: Notice how you walk in their ordinances. You notice how he talks about their ordinances in verse number three of Leviticus 18
point_summary: Leviticus 18:3's contrast between "their ordinances" and God's ordinances (v. 4) grounds the call for God's people to resist cultural conformity in every generation, because God's Word is eternally relevant.

context: but then talks about mine ordinances in Leviticus 18, verse number four
point_summary: God's ordinances in verse 4 stand in deliberate contrast to the pagan ordinances of verse 3, establishing that obedience belongs exclusively to what God commands, not to surrounding cultural norms.

context: as we talked about the golden censer, that as he would on that day of atonement
point_summary: On the Day of Atonement the high priest exchanged his glorious garments for plain linen, a detail the sermon draws as a parallel to Christ setting aside his eternal glory in the incarnation.

context: we come to verses six and seven would deal with this annual day of atonement
point_summary: On the annual Day of Atonement, the high priest alone entered the holy of holies once a year with blood offered for himself and the people, a pattern Hebrews 9 connects to Christ's once-for-all atoning work.

context: we come to verses six and seven would deal with this annual day of atonement
point_summary: On the annual Day of Atonement, the high priest alone entered the Holy of Holies once a year with blood, a restriction that prefigures Christ's singular, atoning entrance into the true presence of God.

context: We don't have time to go back to Leviticus 25, but if you go back there you can read through that chapter and you can see a number of times in Leviticus 25 where God specifically says that they should not make bondmen of their brethren.
point_summary: Leviticus 25 is cited as the legal backdrop prohibiting Israelites from enslaving fellow Israelites, making the intended captivity of Judah a clear violation of God's own law.

context: Look in Leviticus chapter number 20. I use that example because we understand that Jesus was confronted with that in John chapter number eight.
point_summary: Leviticus 20:10 is cited as evidence that certain Mosaic offenses—adultery among them—carried a mandatory death penalty with no atoning sacrifice available, illustrating the severity invoked in Hebrews 10:28.

context: Notice in chapter 20 in verse number 10, and the man that commit adultery with another man's wife, even he that committeth adultery with his neighbor's wife, notice what it says, the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.
point_summary: Leviticus 20:10 is cited to establish that adultery was a capital offense under Mosaic law—an offense for which no atoning sacrifice existed—providing the legal backdrop for John 8.

context: We read it in the book of Leviticus
point_summary: The holiness of Scripture derives from its source — a holy God — as confirmed by the divine self-declaration "I am holy" found in Leviticus and echoed in 1 Peter.

context: What happened when the boys tried to offer some strange fire in the Old Testament? They died.
point_summary: Nadab and Abihu's death for offering strange fire illustrates that God is holy and determines how he is to be worshiped — worship not offered on his terms is unacceptable.

context: if you stand back and look in Leviticus chapter number 16 and understand all of the details surrounding that which would be done on that particular day
point_summary: The Day of Atonement ritual in Leviticus 16 is cited as the backdrop to Hebrews 10, demonstrating that the entire sacrificial system was inadequate—unable to remove sin, only to remember it.

context: and then we'll skip down and look at verses 18 through 22.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And the Lord spake unto Moses in Mount Sinai, saying
point_summary: The divine origin of the Sabbath-year command is underscored by its explicit attribution to God speaking at Sinai, distinguishing it as God's instruction rather than merely human leadership.

context: When ye come into the land which I give you, then shall the land keep a Sabbath unto the Lord.
point_summary: God's word to Moses at Sinai concerning the Sabbath year is presented as divine instruction—not human leadership—establishing that the commands governing the land belong to God alone.

context: We'll read down through verse number 7.
point_summary: Leviticus 25:1–7 establishes God's plan for the seventh-year Sabbath of the land, framing the entire passage as divine instruction—not human leadership—given to Israel upon entering Canaan.

context: Then I will command My blessing upon you in the sixth year, and it shall bring forth fruit for three years.
point_summary: God's promise in the Sabbath-year legislation — "I will command My blessing upon you" — frames the entire message as a study of divine provision contingent on Israel's obedience and faith.

context: When ye come into the land which I give you, then shall the land keep a Sabbath unto the Lord.
point_summary: God's certainty in the phrase "when ye come into the land" — not "if" — is treated as a declaration of divine promise that Israel's entry was guaranteed, not conditional.

context: for six years, you sow, you prune, and you gather the fruit
point_summary: The six-year cycle of sowing, pruning, and gathering in Leviticus 25:3 establishes the normal pattern of agricultural labor that the seventh-year Sabbath rest then deliberately interrupts.

context: But in the seventh year shall be a Sabbath of rest unto the land
point_summary: The seventh year's Sabbath rest for the land extends the creation and weekly Sabbath pattern into an agricultural cycle, requiring Israel to cease sowing, pruning, and reaping as an act of covenant obedience to God.

context: Thou shalt neither sow thy field nor prune thy vineyard.
point_summary: The seventh year's Sabbath rest for the land mirrors the creation pattern and the weekly Sabbath, extending God's established rhythm of work and rest into Israel's agricultural calendar.

context: that which groweth of its own accord of thy harvest thou shalt not reap.
point_summary: In the sabbatical year, even self-grown harvest was to go ungathered, extending the land's complete rest to every category of produce without exception.

context: And the Sabbath of the land shall be meet for you, for thee and for thy servant...
point_summary: During the Sabbath year, whatever the land produces on its own is not wasted but designated as provision for all—servant, hired worker, stranger, and cattle alike.

context: when we come to Leviticus 25 and we see the instruction for when you come into the land
point_summary: Leviticus 25's land-use instructions, given during the wilderness years, illustrate that God was actively preparing Israel for future obedience before they ever entered the land.

context: God gives in Leviticus 25 verses 1 through 7
point_summary: God's preparation of Israel through the wilderness wanderings equipped them for the obedience required in the Sabbath-year instructions of Leviticus 25, illustrating how consistent daily time in God's Word prepares believers for future trials.

context: God gives in Leviticus 25 verses 1 through 7
point_summary: God's provision of manna during the wilderness wanderings prepared Israel to trust him in the obedience of the seventh-year sabbath rest commanded in Leviticus 25:1–7.

context: as we look at these verses in Leviticus 25, verses 18-22
point_summary: Obedience to God's statutes and judgments in the sabbath-year law carries the explicit promise of safety and provision, framing Leviticus 25:18 as the foundation for embracing God's covenant blessing over human anxiety about need.

context: I will command my blessing upon you in the sixth year, and it shall bring forth fruit for three years.
point_summary: God's promise to multiply the sixth-year harvest for three years demanded active faith that He would do exactly what He said, mirroring the same trust required with the manna.

context: And ye shall sow the eighth year, and eat yet of old fruit until the ninth year; until her fruits come in ye shall eat of the old store.
point_summary: Leviticus 25:22 illustrates that obeying God's sabbath-year command required active faith that His promised sixth-year blessing would sustain the people through years seven and eight until the eighth-year harvest came in.

context: But in the seventh year shall be a sabbath of rest unto the land, a sabbath for the LORD: thou shalt neither sow thy field, nor prune thy vineyard.
point_summary: Observing the seventh-year sabbath of the land demanded active faith that God's commanded blessing in year six would supernaturally sustain Israel through years seven and eight without sowing.

context: ...I am in absolutely no way trying to tell you that the text of Leviticus 25 is to be applied in our lives today from a perspective of agriculture...
point_summary: Israel's 70-year Babylonian exile is presented as the land's enforced collection of 490 years' worth of neglected Sabbath rests, demonstrating that God always fulfills His word when His people fail to trust and obey His plan.

context: And we see that here in Leviticus 25, this truth is given.
point_summary: Israel's 490-year failure to observe the Sabbath year reveals that Leviticus 25 establishes a principle—not an agricultural mandate for today—that trusting and obeying God's plan brings His promised blessing.

reference_text: be ye holy as I am holy
point_summary: God's holiness, repeatedly declared in Leviticus with the command "be ye holy as I am holy," grounds the attitude of reverence that the word "hallowed" calls believers to bring into prayer.

reference_text: be holy even as I am holy
context: he refers back to that passage or those passages in Leviticus when he says be holy even as I am holy
point_summary: Leviticus's repeated command "be ye holy as I am holy" is cited as scriptural grounding for the holiness of God, establishing that prayer must be approached with reverence toward a holy Father.

context: Thou shalt love thy neighbor as thyself
point_summary: Loving one's neighbor as oneself, cited as "the royal law" from Scripture, is presented as the divine standard that expels partiality and defines love as truth-giving rather than mere accommodation.

context: ye shall be holy, for I the Lord your God am holy
point_summary: God's call to holiness in Leviticus 19:2 grounds the prohibition against partiality in verse 15, since a holy God who is no respecter of persons demands the same impartiality from His people.

context: ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment. Thou shalt not respect the person of the poor nor honor the person of the mighty, but in righteousness shalt thou judge thy neighbor
point_summary: Leviticus 19:15 is cited as an Old Testament principle establishing that God's people must judge without partiality toward poor or powerful alike, grounding the command in God's own holy character.

context: go to the book of Leviticus if you will Leviticus chapter number 26
point_summary: Leviticus 26 is cited to establish that famine as divine judgment is a biblical pattern predating Revelation, with God explicitly linking land's failed yield to covenantal disobedience.

context: and your labour shall be spent in vain, for your land shall not yield her increase, neither shall the trees of the land yield their fruits
point_summary: Leviticus 26:20 is cited to establish that famine throughout Scripture signals divine judgment, with God sovereignly withholding harvest as a consequence of disobedience to his commandments.

context: but if you will not hearken unto me and will not do all these commandments
point_summary: Leviticus 26:14 is cited as the conditional warning introducing God's covenant curses, establishing that famine is a direct consequence of disobedience and therefore an instrument of divine judgment.

context: we see in Leviticus 26, as God is preparing his people for them to have this understanding that famine reveals divine judgment
point_summary: Leviticus 26:20 is read as God's covenant warning that withheld harvests are not agricultural failure but divine judgment on disobedience, establishing that famine reveals God's sovereign control.

context: the book of Leviticus chapter four, it's repeated four times in that chapter, verse seven
point_summary: The repeated Levitical command to pour the sacrificial blood at the base of the altar grounds the imagery of Revelation 6:9, where martyred souls appear "under the altar" — their shed blood locating them there.

context: verse 18
point_summary: The repeated Old Testament command to pour blood at the altar's base (Lev. 4:7, 18, 25, 30) grounds the imagery of martyred souls appearing "under the altar" in Revelation 6:9, linking their shed blood to sacrificial offering.

context: verse 25
point_summary: The repeated Old Testament prescription to pour blood at the base of the altar (Lev. 4:7, 18, 25, 30) grounds the imagery of martyred souls appearing "under the altar" in Revelation 6:9.

context: verse number 30
point_summary: The repeated Levitical command to pour blood at the base of the altar grounds the imagery of martyred souls appearing "under the altar" in Revelation 6:9, connecting Old Testament sacrificial blood-placement to the location of the slain saints.

context: You read the book of Leviticus, and you'll read it repeatedly throughout the book of Leviticus, where God says to his people, be holy. Why? Because I am holy.
point_summary: God's repeated command in Leviticus — "Be holy, because I am holy" — establishes the foundational character of God as holy, which James draws on to frame why God cannot be the source of sinful temptation.

context: be holy as I am holy
point_summary: God's holiness—repeatedly declared in Leviticus as the basis for his command "be holy"—is the character trait James invokes to rule out any notion that God could be the source of evil temptation.

context: the Bible talks about sin being in the camp, and it affects the whole
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we're to be holy as he is holy, referencing the book of Leviticus
point_summary: God's call to holiness—referenced through Leviticus as the standard for the church's purity—underscores that holy living remains essential, not optional, for believers.

context: it's first mentioned in the book of Leviticus with regard to God saying to His people, be holy, for I am holy
point_summary: The holiness of God, first declared in Leviticus as the basis for His people's own holy conduct, is the same attribute the four living creatures proclaim ceaselessly before the throne.

context: because it is written, be holy, for I am holy, Leviticus
point_summary: Peter's quotation of Leviticus 11:44 grounds the call to holy living in God's own character, showing that the holiness declared by the four living creatures in heaven is the same standard God requires of His people in every area of behavior.

context: it's in Leviticus 17, verses 11 through 14, and we're not going to take the time to read those verses, but you can jot that down, where the Bible tells us there that the the life of the flesh is in the blood
point_summary: The first occurrence of "life" in Scripture (Genesis 1:20) is linked to Leviticus 17:11–14's declaration that the life of the flesh is in the blood, showing how God begins to connect these themes from day five of creation onward.

context: if you obey my voice, here's all the things that I'm going to do. And if you disobey my voice, here's all the things that are gonna happen
point_summary: Obedience to God's revealed instruction brings blessing, while Israel's rejection of that instruction in favor of their own counsel illustrates the perpetual danger of explaining away God's word rather than hearkening to it.

context: if you read the book of Leviticus, God tells them, if you disobey, I'm gonna spew you out of the land
point_summary: Leviticus warned Israel explicitly that disobedience would result in expulsion from the promised land—a warning the sermon treats as literally and historically fulfilled in the Assyrian and Babylonian captivities.

context: I'm going to invite you to go back to Leviticus 23
point_summary: Leviticus 23 is introduced as the foundational text for understanding the Feast of Tabernacles, providing the background necessary to interpret Psalm 81's association with that feast.

context: in Leviticus 23. Look if you will, begin reading with me in verse 33. The Bible says this, And the Lord spake unto Moses, saying, Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, The fifteenth day of this seventh month shall be the Feast of Tabernacles
point_summary: Leviticus 23:33 is read as the foundational text establishing the Feast of Tabernacles—its timing, duration, and sacred convocations—to illuminate the psalm associated with it.

context: On the eighth day shall be a holy convocation unto you, and ye shall offer an offering made by fire unto the Lord
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: These are the feasts of the Lord, which ye shall proclaim to be holy convocations, to offer an offering made by fire unto the Lord
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Also in the fifteenth day of the seventh month, when ye have gathered in the fruit of the land
point_summary: Leviticus 23:39 establishes the Feast of Tabernacles as a seven-day harvest celebration—also called the Feast of Ingathering—marked by rejoicing before the Lord with branches and boughs.

context: When you gather in the fruit of the land, ye shall keep a feast unto the Lord seven days. On the first day shall be a Sabbath, and on the eighth day shall be a Sabbath, and ye shall take you on the first day the boughs of goodly trees, branches of palm trees, and the boughs of thick trees, and willows of the brook, and ye shall rejoice before the Lord your God seven days.
point_summary: Leviticus 23:39 identifies the Feast of Tabernacles as a seven-day harvest celebration beginning on the fifteenth of the seventh month, also called the Feast of Ingathering.

context: and ye shall keep a feast unto the Lord seven days in the year. It shall be a statute forever in your generations. Ye shall celebrate it in the seventh month.
point_summary: The Feast of Tabernacles is established as a perpetual, annually recurring statute, observed in the seventh month, binding on all generations of Israel.

context: ye shall dwell in booths seven days. All that are Israelites born shall dwell in booths, that your generations may know that I made the children of Israel to dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt.
point_summary: The booth-dwelling command was given so that future generations would remember God's provision and deliverance of Israel out of Egypt, embedding historical memory into annual observance.

context: And Moses declared unto the children of Israel the feast of the Lord. Verse 44 can almost seem like it's a little bit insignificant
point_summary: Verse 44's significance lies in Moses's faithful obedience, delivering to Israel exactly the feast ordinances God had entrusted to him—a pattern of ministerial faithfulness the sermon highlights.

context: as we look here at Leviticus 23, we read a bulk of information with regard to the feast of tabernacles
point_summary: Leviticus 23's detailed prescription for the Feast of Tabernacles—dwelling in booths, rejoicing seven days, and Moses faithfully delivering God's command—provides the foundational background for understanding Psalm 81's association with this feast.

context: verses 34 through 36 here in Leviticus 23 that we read a moment ago
point_summary: Leviticus 23:34 anchors the Feast of Tabernacles as one of three annual feasts requiring all Jewish males to appear before the Lord, with its seven-day duration and eighth-day convocation observed through dwelling in booths.

context: ye shall dwell in booths seven days
point_summary: Dwelling in booths during the Feast of Tabernacles was God's intentional reminder to Israel, even after settling in permanent houses, of their wilderness pilgrimage origins.

context: God tells them to do that in verse 42
point_summary: The command to dwell in booths was given so that successive generations would remember God's provision and deliverance of Israel out of Egypt, not merely as a one-time observance but as a recurring, embodied act of remembrance across time.

context: in verse 43, notice what he says, that your generations may know
point_summary: The booth-dwelling command was designed not merely for one generation's memory but to transmit across successive generations the concrete historical reality of God's deliverance of Israel from Egypt.

context: your generations may know that I made the children of Israel to dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt. I am the Lord your God
point_summary: God commanded the Feast of Tabernacles not merely for those who experienced the exodus, but so that future generations—children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren—would know what He had done for Israel.

context: and ye shall rejoice before the Lord your God seven days
point_summary: The branches and boughs gathered in Leviticus 23:40 were instruments of seven days' rejoicing before God, celebrating His provision of the harvest as the ultimate source of their abundance.

context: according to what we read in Leviticus and also in Exodus
point_summary: The Feast of Tabernacles required every Jewish male to go up to Jerusalem, a legal obligation that placed Jesus—as a Jewish man—in direct tension with the threat on his life.

context: if we understand what we read in Leviticus 23, all of these things that are happening
point_summary: The Feast of Tabernacles' final day, as prescribed in Leviticus 23, provides the liturgical backdrop for Jesus' cry in John 7:37, where the priestly water-pouring ritual illuminates his offer of living water.

context: this book of Leviticus, Exodus, this feast that the children of Israel to observe
point_summary: The Feast of Tabernacles in Leviticus was not merely a historical observance for Israel but a prophetic type pointing thousands of years forward to the literal millennial reign of Christ.

context: Later in the book of Leviticus, God would give us further detail with regard to these burnt offerings
point_summary: Burnt offerings, first mentioned in Genesis 8 and later detailed in Leviticus, are identified as the means by which an unholy people could approach a holy God, picturing atonement for sin.

context: we look in the book of Leviticus and understand the description that is given to us
point_summary: The burnt offerings described later in Leviticus illuminate Noah's sacrifice in Genesis 8 as a picture of atonement, showing how an unholy people could approach a holy God.

context: the strange fire that was brought they were killed
point_summary: Unauthorized worship invites divine judgment, as seen in the "strange fire" of Leviticus 10:1, underscoring that God is holy and must be approached only as He has prescribed.

context: prior to when Moses wrote those in Leviticus 18, 19, and 20
point_summary: Leviticus 18–20 is cited as the point in redemptive history—roughly 2,500 years after Cain—when God first formally prohibited marriage between close relatives, meaning no such law existed in Cain's day.

context: prior to when Moses wrote those in Leviticus 18, 19, and 20
point_summary: Leviticus 18–20 is cited as the point in redemptive history—approximately 2,500 years after Cain—when God first formally prohibited marriage between close relatives, explaining why no such law existed to condemn Cain.

context: prior to when Moses wrote those in Leviticus 18, 19, and 20
point_summary: Leviticus 18–20 is cited as the point in redemptive history—roughly 2,500 years after Cain—when God first formally prohibited marriage between close relatives, explaining why no such law existed in Cain's day.

context: as you look at even the laws that God gave in Leviticus 18 through 20
point_summary: The laws in Leviticus 18 prohibiting marriage to close relatives were given approximately 2,500 years after Cain's time, meaning no such prohibition existed when Cain married his sister.

context: we went back to Leviticus and to Exodus
point_summary: The feasts prescribed in Leviticus were not arbitrary religious customs but divinely ordained statutes — permanent rules issued by God as supreme authority for the good of his people.

context: read the book of Leviticus you read the book of Exodus
point_summary: The feasts prescribed in Leviticus are identified as both a "statute" — a specific prescribed task — and a permanent "law" issued by God as supreme authority over his people.

context: turn over to Leviticus chapter number 9 if you will
point_summary: The Levitical sin offering—requiring blood, not grain—is cited to demonstrate that God's prescribed standard for atonement was always blood-specific, vindicating Abel's offering over Cain's.

context: In Leviticus 9 in verse number 3 when the instruction would be given it says this, under the children of Israel thou shalt speak saying, taking a kid of the goats for a sin offering and a calf and a lamb both of the first year without blemish for a burnt offering
point_summary: Leviticus 9:3's requirement of a blood offering — a kid, calf, and unblemished lamb — is cited as evidence that God had communicated to Adam a specific standard requiring blood, not grain, for the sin offering.

context: we looked at the book of Leviticus we find that many of the specifics of the
point_summary: God's verbal instruction to Adam and Eve—predating the written Word—established the requirement for acceptable offering, a pattern later codified in Leviticus with specific detail.

context: you read the book of Leviticus and you read through all of the details
point_summary: Leviticus's exhaustive, specific regulations for offerings represent the later written codification of a sacrificial requirement God had already communicated verbally long before the law, tracing back to Eden itself.

context: the pattern that was so specifically given through the book of Leviticus
point_summary: Leviticus codified in detailed written form the specific requirements for offerings that God had already communicated orally to Adam, establishing the pattern of acceptable sacrifice long before the law.

context: I thought it necessary to go back and to study in the book of Leviticus
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we can look at the book of Leviticus as well
point_summary: Leviticus, read alongside Exodus 29–30, reinforces that priestly washing pictures salvation as the necessary first step before any consecration or service can follow.

context: Go to the book of Leviticus, if you will, in chapter 18. Leviticus chapter 18.
point_summary: Leviticus 18 is read as God's program to purge Egypt—the world—out of His people after He had already brought them out of Egypt, a pattern the sermon applies to ongoing Christian separation.

context: So notice what he says in Leviticus 18. It's a great principle that's given in verse number one. The Lord spake unto Moses saying, speak unto the children of Israel and say unto them, I am the Lord your God.
point_summary: Leviticus 18:1 introduces the principle that God's people must reject the practices of both the culture they left and the culture they are entering, conforming instead to God's own ordinances.

context: Notice in verse three, a two-fold principle that is given. He says this, after the doings of the land of Egypt, wherein ye dwelt, shall ye not do. And after the doings of the land of Canaan, whither I bring you, shall ye not do. Neither shall you walk in their ordinances.
point_summary: Leviticus 18:3 establishes a twofold separation principle: God's people were to reject the practices of both Egypt behind them and Canaan before them, conforming instead to God's own ordinances.

context: Ye shall do my judgments and keep mine ordinances, to walk therein. I am the Lord your God. Ye shall therefore keep my statutes and my judgments. Notice this, which if a man do, he shall live in them. I am the Lord.
point_summary: Leviticus 18:4 grounds the call to biblical separation in God's positive demand—not merely avoiding Egypt's or Canaan's practices, but actively doing His judgments and keeping His ordinances, living by His way rather than the world's.

context: Leviticus chapter number 16 addresses, and we won't take the time to read the text, but in Leviticus chapter 16, you'll find that specifically what is addressed
point_summary: Leviticus 16, the Day of Atonement, is cited as part of the Mosaic sacrificial system that, alongside the Passover, pointed forward typologically to the redemptive work of the coming Messiah.

context: when we kind of zero in specifically on chapter 16, it's dealing with the day of atonement
point_summary: In Leviticus 16's Day of Atonement legislation, God's name appears as "Jehovah" twelve times, linking His covenant name directly to the redemptive work that Christ, the sinless High Priest, would ultimately fulfill.

context: the book of Leviticus in the 20th chapter would make it plain that both parties would be guilty of being stoned
point_summary: Leviticus 20's requirement that both parties in adultery be stoned exposes the accusers' selective enforcement against the woman alone, underscoring the hypocrisy Jesus confronted before declaring himself the light of the world.

context: Nadab and Abihu, did they find out that there was a certain way to approach God, and there was a certain way that they shouldn't approach God? Did the people find out that if you offer strange fire, it's a problem? Sure they did. They were killed, right?
point_summary: The deaths of Nadab and Abihu for offering strange fire establish that God alone determines the acceptable terms of approach, not human culture or preference.

context: Leviticus chapter number 10 in verses 8-11
point_summary: Because Jesus serves as our great high priest, the Levitical prohibition against priests drinking wine or strong drink in the tabernacle bears directly on whether he would have consumed alcoholic beverages.

context: and the Lord spake unto Aaron
point_summary: Because Jesus serves as our great high priest, the Levitical prohibition against priests drinking wine during tabernacle service (Lev. 10:8–9) is cited as evidence that he would not have consumed alcoholic wine.

context: Do not drink wine nor strong drink thou nor thy sons with thee when you go into the tabernacle of the congregation lest ye die
point_summary: Because Jesus fulfills the Aaronic priesthood's prohibition on wine as a high priest perpetually in service—never off duty—he would never have consumed alcoholic wine.
